narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 2.90 and an LSAT score of 35, you've put together a respectable profile for law school, including plans to attend full-time and coming from a middle-income family background. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated your probability of failure at 61.6%—high enough that it tipped the decision toward rejecting your application to protect the school's bar passage rates. The primary driver pushing the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, which placed you in the bottom tenth of your peers that year, far below the typical average of around the 5th or 6th decile. This low performance in your later undergraduate years stood out as the most influential factor, signaling to the model a potential struggle with sustained academic rigor needed for law school and the bar. Counterbalancing that somewhat was your LSAT score, which pulled the prediction in a positive direction toward passing, as it's close to the dataset average of 36.7 and shows decent standardized test ability. Your undergraduate GPA also helped a bit, nudging things toward success since your 2.90 is reasonably near the average of 3.21, reflecting consistent prior effort. Compared to typical students who get approved, your early Year 1 decile of 2 was similarly low, but it was the sharper drop-off in Year 3 that set you apart from those with steadier upward trajectories. While factors like your full-time status (common for 93% of students) and family income didn't sway things much, the model weighed your grades trajectory most heavily. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from a middle-class background—many in similar situations succeed with targeted improvements. To move forward, focus on boosting your academic deciles through retakes, additional coursework, or test prep to demonstrate upward momentum, perhaps retaking the LSAT for a higher score. Consider gaining practical experience like internships to strengthen your narrative, and reapply after addressing these gaps—schools value resilience and growth. With deliberate steps, you can shift that prediction in your favor next time.
